item.title: Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
The first image is all rights reserved.

Second image is all rights reserved.

Last image is also all rights reserved.

None of these images are compliant with CC 3.0 and as such have been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What snake is getting at is that in-universe, any reasonable scp article would include a summary of terms.
This has held up pretty well. A recommended read.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really enjoy this one, but one irregularity confuses me. You mention a scroll written in the aftermath of the fall of the Daevan empire, circa 9000 BCE. However, all other articles mention the empire lasting until either 200 BCE or the 19th century, depending on the timeline. What gives?

I know there is no oficial canon and so things like this are to be expected :) I just hope to understand better what you meant by those writings, because they are very interesting. In overall, this is a great SCP. I'd love to eventually see some exploration logs, or more written texts found within.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Mu. I've just been watching Monster Rancher, so that name has a different meaning for me and adds an interesting twist to this scp.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
[Illegible] was here.

There's only one being I can think of with the stones to do something this ballsy.

Saturn Deer was here.

I feex.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think this article does stand on its own. It has elements that weave into a larger tapestry (the Daevic civilization), but if you removed those elements (or substituted something real-world, even), it would still work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Out of universe reason: links to super popular Series I articles are a downvote magnet, perhaps the author didn't want this to fail for entirely arbitrary reasons. I know that's why SCP-2140 doesn't use the D-word and redacts the link to 140…

Both in and out of universe, there is no reason for these links to not be brought up in the article. People should be able to understand what's going on by reading this article alone, which is not the case.

It does have the Daevite tag, though. Did you try that?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I didn't count 140 simply because I haven't read it yet. That's my problem with this entry. It relies too much on the reader being already familiar with that canon and doesn't even mention the fact that traces of the Daevic empire have been found in other SCPs.

Both in and out of universe, there is no reason for these links to not be brought up in the article. People should be able to understand what's going on by reading this article alone, which is not the case. Again, the article doesn't even make it obvious that there are other records of that civilisation, making it very confusing that the SCP foundation would just assume that it's real and treat it as such.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This SCP being the only source of records on this civilization, the article really should be explaining what makes it distinguishable.

Why aren't you counting SCP-140?

If the Daevic civilization is referred to in other SCPs (which I suspect they are), then it really should be mentioned and again, the article should still give at least a short explaination of Daevic culture

Did you try the tag?
http://www.scp-wiki.net/system:page-tags/tag/daevite#pages

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's interesting but something about it prevents me from liking it enough to upvote. I feel like there are elements that should be better explained as they are written as if the reader is expected to be familiar with them despite them only existing within the confines of this SCP.

For example, the Daevic empire. The article acts like they're a commonly known and well-established fact and that anyone reading the article should know what that culture was like. They keep referring to various items as "Daevic" without ever explaining the characteristics of Daevic culture which could have led them to this conclusion, or how they even know that the culture is real. This SCP being the only source of records on this civilization, the article really should be explaining what makes it distinguishable.

In fact, there are quite a few things that the Foundation just seems to be familiar with without us being given any indication that they should be. How do they know that the Daevic eternal engine is what it is, especially if it isn't functioning? If the Daevic civilization is referred to in other SCPs (which I suspect they are), then it really should be mentioned and again, the article should still give at least a short explaination of Daevic culture. As it stands, it feels like the SCP Foundation just somehow knows about it despite there being no indication that they do.

There are just too many things in this article that are accepted without question and it feels like the reader is expected to do the same and as a result, it feels a bit too obtuse for my taste.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My only gripe here is the following line:

SCP-1726-1-03, SCP-1726-1-20, and SCP-1726-1-44 are fluent in English.

Matter of headcanon, I guess, but I don't think this would be particularly relevant to a worldwide organization like the Foundation. Or perhaps this only applies to this version of the document, with the localized versions pointing out which instances know the language.

Anyways, this was pretty good. Not much happened, but the imagery is awesome and I have a feeling that if I go through your tales I'll find more than a few callbacks to this place.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Curse you, Sinic language family! Curse youuuuuuuuuuuu!

But really, thanks for the heads up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The single included Chinese phrase "Wǒ guàng dào hěn dà de jù, wǒ xuéhuìle wànshì" is quite poor in quality (in fact, the SCP-CN translator practically chose to ignore that phrase because it was cringeworthy).

One, the phrase is grammatically incorrect (suggesting the use of online translation tools).

Two, the phrase is tonally similar to modern Vernacular Chinese instead of the more appropriate Classical Chinese (more evidence suggesting the use of online translation tools).

I suggest that the author should just remove the Chinese phrase and leave the translation in. (I can't offer any replacements because I am not that good with Classical Chinese myself.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is the beast referenced here SCP-169?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't like that the article seems to refer, without qualification or explanation, to things that for all the Foundation knows may well be fictional. I mean, these aren't things there are any real historical precedences for, so it's a bit strange for the Foundation just to assume what these books say are perfectly factual.

Like, for example, there is no such thing as an Eternal Engine (I don't know why it's capitalised) outside of fiction, so how does the Foundation know it's not just a useless contraption, or another scip?

I mean, apart from that, this is fantastic — that's just something that bothered me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Actually, no.

Lemuria is a fictional continent spanning the Indian ocean, supposedly connecting the landmasses of Madagascar, Australia, and India. Like Atlantis, it was sunken by a cataclysm of some kind.

The More You Know.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Lemurian."

Destroyermen series reference?

Regardless, nice job. Love the nods to SCP-140, too.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Okay, 12.8 kilometers is a bit more notable. I still want to know what they experienced up there, though. Maybe an exploration log is in order?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Kilometers, man. 12.8 kilometers.

Unfamiliarity with Pratchett is a capital offense, punishable by Death.

This is not a reference to Discworld, I was merely using it as an example. It is, however, filled with references to other things of the scip universe.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So, it has a stairway to heaven? :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not that familiar with Pratchett, sorry, and Googling the quote didn't help. If this is just a bunch of references to other things, I should maybe reconsider my upvote.

I still feel like something's missing from the pillar, though. I mean, agents climbed up almost 13 meters and then, what, just gave up? You can't see it from outside the structure, but what can you see from the pillar? (Also, I just noticed that the unknowability of its height is mentioned twice; I'd remove the first instance.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
:|

I reject Mack's definition and substitute my own, without actually doing that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: With Mack's retagging, it seems you've just narrowly lost. Kind of a bummer, 'cause I really thought this was gonna win.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fixed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The path leading SCP-1726

Shouldn't this be "leading to"?

A stone pillar rises out of the central garden of SCP-1726, ascending through a hole in the roof to an unknown height. This pillar is climbable by means of a wooden walkway spiraling around the edge of the pillar. This pillar exists within the spatial anomaly of SCP-1726, and so is not able to be seen from the outside. The exact height of the pillar is unknown: the highest point reached by Foundation agents is 12.8 km.

You've got five instances of the word "pillar" in four successive sentences, which feels a wee bit repetitive.

It also feels a bit weird to have a Greek helmet and Michelangelo's David in the background of the second picture when the description specifically mentions Asian artefacts, but that's hardly noticeable. :)

But overall, I love lost civilisations, dizzying heights, and this article. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There is a wonderful Google search bar built right into your internet browser.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I expand this to not being just all libraries, but all places containing information. It's just that the connection becomes easier to traverse the more information there is, and the more it is shared between the two locations. This is the mechanism behind a number of forms of telepathy, for instance.

A sufficiently wise individual would be able to pull himself into the Library through sheer will, but there's virtually never been anyone that knowledgeable. The best people can usually do is pull themselves into a realm of even greater concentration of knowledge than the Library, which they generally don't return from.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's kind of like my personal theory on infinite libraries in differing works, that they all connect. Someone in the wanderer's library might meet an orangutan (not a monkey, do not call him a monkey) shelving books for example. Or in a collection of cassette decks and moldering paperback Star Trek novels in SCP-967 suddenly turning into a Way.

Also, I like the whole attempt at a backstory for multiple cultures. Seems like this is just a door to the library to me. Not fully open, which explains why rather than normal mutated thieves and/or people who had overdue books serving as librarians it has a bunch of low-quality worker golems bought in some alternative reality the library links to.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'd just like to say that I put that second picture (of the statue holding the peach) on one of the "hey, this picture would make a good SCP" pages literally YEARS* ago. And it makes me happy to see it actually used on a page.

It's possible that I would have upvoted it just for that, but luckily, this being a good article saves me from having to worry about that. Good show.

*Just checked, it was March 2009. This very month! Spooky.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Forgive my ignorance, but what is Mu?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this.

for an elongated period of time

Should this be "an extended period of time"?

Regardless, upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fixed the mainlist name

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's missing a name on the main SCP series.

I like the pillar, reminds me of the story of the Monkey King and the Buddha.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yep. These automatons aren't officially part of the hierarchy. This is more of a branch.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So wait, this is an entrance to that place1? I love this idea. Shame the automatons don't quite fit in their heirarchy. You would figure the Foundation would have recognized one'a their servants, though.

Footnotes
1. yeah, that one, you know what I'm talkin' 'bout

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, I feel like that if you're something tied to libraries, like the Library servant, or possibly even a dedicated public librarian, you'd get to the Library through climbing it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No, there's significance. Consider the nature of the place, the nature of the creature seen, the lantern, and the wise words of our friend Terry Pratchett on libraries.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I, too, would like more info on the central pillar. Climbing it helps you escape the feelings of extraspatial nausea, but beyond that, there seems to be no significance to it.

The thing I like most about this is the very matter-of-fact way things like the fall of Mu are reported. "Yeah, there's this library in China, full of books about Mu and the Daevics and a bunch of other civilizations that shouldn't exist, and it's fourteen square kilometers inside! Can you believe that?"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Love it, love it, love it.

It's got a lot of intriguing hooks without becoming self-indulgent with a giant log of "let's overexplain everything," which I think it could easily have been. Also, I'm a sucker for repositories of knowledge about lost history.

Dig the Chinese angle, too. Adds just the right flair of personality to it all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'd like more information on that central pillar, but I like it regardless.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Aye, that is correct, but they do lend themselves to certain things I have written previously on the subject.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: All those lines mentioning the Daevic Empire are references to SCP-140, of course. But that's not exactly hard to pick out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
:D

Glad to help. That said, I do like this one. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Many thanks to Zyn for helping me with the translation on that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good job, I like this. I'm also really happy that you used proper pinyin.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ding ding ding, cookie dispensing time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is the type of SCPs I like to see, hinting at a larger picture while still being complete enough in their own write to be interesting. I think it might be a bit too focused on the non-existent and all too ancient societies (maybe a bit of real history would be nice as well), but it's nice regardless.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I want to see more here, but on the whole I'm good. Real nifty.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Very nicely done. One question tho:

██/██/20██: An unknown entity was observed by researchers described as a squat humanoid with limbs similar to those of an arachnid. The entity was seen re-arranging books, and upon being seen fled from researchers and ascended the central pillar. General research within SCP-1726 has been halted for the time being.

Is this a Library reference?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it. As I told you earlier, I still think it could use with a bit of overall fleshing out. I know you added the new parts to do that, but now I'm left with a feeling like it could do with a bit more tying together, even though I know what it's suppose to be.

That being said, I think this might end up being the first successful entry to the challenge. I'm a bit worried about whether or not Knowledge is adequately fulfilled, but let's keep our fingers crossed!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In response to Wilt's Challenge. Got the third picture from him. Tags were automaton, portal, and knowledge.

e: internet cookies to those who can parse all the references.
